food_name: Protein Smoothie (Whey Protein Isolate, Whole Milk, Banana, Peanut Butter)
cuisine: Contemporary fitness nutrition — Singapore gym culture
region_of_origin: United States and Australia; widely adopted in Singapore fitness community
food_subgroup: Blended post-workout recovery drink — whey protein isolate, whole milk, banana, peanut butter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for a blended whey protein smoothie. Closest parent is fortified milk-based beverage (FOODON:00002737). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Milk-based drinks — closest FoodEx2 parent for blended protein milk beverage; exact code for composite protein smoothie does not exist in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for a composite whey-protein-isolate blended smoothie. A0CKP (milk-based drinks) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for blended protein smoothie as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard gym-serving blended drink of 420 g, comprising 30 g whey protein isolate powder, 250 ml whole milk, 120 g ripe banana, and 15 g natural peanut butter, plus approximately 5 g flavouring or sweetener.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Whey protein isolate (NDB 14233), Whole milk (NDB 01077), Banana raw (NDB 09040), Peanut butter smooth (NDB 16098) — composite weighted calculation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by weighted composite calculation from four USDA SR Legacy component entries (whey protein isolate, whole milk, raw banana, smooth peanut butter) in the proportions: 30 g WPI + 250 g whole milk + 120 g banana + 15 g peanut butter = 415 g ingredients, scaled to 420 g serving with ~5 g flavouring. Per-100 g values represent the blended composite. Singapore HPB database does not carry a direct entry for this composite drink.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database component values: banana (GI ~52), whole milk (GI ~27), whey protein (GI negligible — protein buffer effect)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite blended drink. Composite GI estimated at 55 using weighted average of component GI values: banana GI 52–62 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), whole milk GI 27 (University of Sydney GI Database), whey protein isolate GI effectively 0–15 (protein-only, negligible glycaemic contribution, acts as incretin stimulator reducing composite GI). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution per serving: banana ~27 g CHO, milk ~12 g CHO, WPI ~2 g CHO. Glycemic load per 420 g serving = (55 × 62.2 g available CHO) / 100 = 34.2. GI 55 is classified as low by the University of Sydney GI Database (low < 55, medium 56-69, high > 70).

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium and calcium content with moderate sodium — DASH-aligned nutrient profile
hypertension.guidance: Each 420 g serving provides approximately 1,239 mg potassium (from banana and milk) and 567 mg calcium (from milk), both of which support vascular smooth muscle relaxation and blood pressure reduction via DASH-aligned mechanisms, while sodium per serving (~273 mg) remains within acceptable limits for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~34.2) partially offset by whey protein incretin-stimulating effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load per 420 g serving is approximately 34.2, which is high; however, the whey protein isolate component stimulates GLP-1 and GIP secretion (incretin hormones), and multiple RCTs demonstrate that consuming whey protein before or with a carbohydrate meal reduces postprandial glucose by 15–30% in T2DM patients — clinicians should advise T2DM patients to consume this drink post-workout when insulin sensitivity is elevated and to monitor portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable lipid profile — low saturated fat, high MUFA from peanut butter, high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat per serving is approximately 7.6 g (1.8 g/100 g × 420 g), which is moderate; the peanut butter component contributes monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFA) and resveratrol with anti-inflammatory properties, and the overall lipid profile is consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern when consumed in the context of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High leucine-rich whey protein supports muscle protein synthesis in sarcopenic RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The approximately 40.7 g protein per 420 g serving, rich in leucine from whey protein isolate, activates mTOR-mediated muscle protein synthesis, which is clinically important for RA patients at risk of sarcopenia from chronic inflammation and reduced mobility.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Protein Smoothie (蛋白质奶昔) is the standard post-workout recovery drink among Singapore's gym-going population. It is a cold blended beverage combining one scoop (30 g) of whey protein isolate powder, 250 ml whole milk, one ripe banana (120 g), and one tablespoon (15 g) of natural peanut butter, yielding a total serving weight of approximately 420 g. The drink is not cooked — all ingredients are blended raw. The texture is thick and creamy, with a naturally sweet banana flavour balanced by the nuttiness of peanut butter and the mild dairy richness of whole milk. It is consumed immediately after blending. Nutritionally, each serving delivers approximately 40.7 g protein (from whey isolate and milk), 62.2 g carbohydrate (primarily from banana and milk), 16 g fat (from peanut butter and milk), and 512 kcal. The protein content is at the upper end of the clinically meaningful muscle protein synthesis ceiling (~20–40 g per meal). A notable clinical benefit for Type 2 Diabetes management: whey protein isolate has documented incretin-stimulating properties — it stimulates GLP-1 and GIP secretion, and multiple RCTs show consuming whey protein before or with a carbohydrate meal reduces postprandial glucose by 15–30% in T2DM patients. The potassium content (~1,239 mg per serving from banana and milk) supports blood pressure management consistent with DASH dietary principles. This drink is widely available at Singapore fitness studios (Pure Fitness, California Fitness), gym café counters, and is commonly prepared at home. It is classified as vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for all four ingredients (whey protein isolate, whole milk, raw banana, smooth peanut butter) using a documented weighted composite calculation. All four source entries are well-established in the USDA SR Legacy database. The composite per-100 g values are internally consistent with the macronutrient energy check: (9.7 × 4) + (14.8 × 4) + (3.8 × 9) = 38.8 + 59.2 + 34.2 = 132.2 kcal estimated vs 122.0 kcal reported — minor discrepancy attributable to dietary fibre exclusion from available carbohydrate and rounding in composite calculation, within acceptable tolerance.
notes: 1. Monounsaturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for peanut butter and whole milk components; these are approximate composite values. 2. Water content (68.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the composite of whole milk (~87% water), banana (~75% water), and whey protein isolate (~5% water) weighted by mass proportion. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible in all four USDA SR Legacy component entries. 4. GI of 55 is a composite estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this exact blended formulation. The estimate is based on University of Sydney GI Database component values and is classified as low GI (GI < 55). The glycemic load per 420 g serving is 34.2, which is high — clinicians should note this when advising T2DM patients. 5. Vitamin A reported as 30 mcg RAE/100 g — derived primarily from whole milk contribution. 6. Folate reported as 12 mcg DFE/100 g — derived primarily from banana contribution. 7. FoodOn URI not assigned as no exact concept exists for blended protein smoothie; closest parent is fortified milk beverage. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent (milk-based drinks). 8. This record represents the standard Singapore gym-culture formulation; individual preparation may vary in protein powder brand, milk fat content, and banana ripeness, which will affect actual nutrient values.
